primary_diagnosis.name: Nodulo-ulcerative basal cell carcinoma with acute haemorrhage

pathophysiology_summary: A basal cell carcinoma on chronically sun-exposed skin (nasal ala) shows classic features: a pearly, translucent, dome-shaped nodule with a rolled border, arborising telangiectasia and central ulceration. Fragile tumour surface vessels bleed with minor trauma (towel drying); antiplatelet therapy prolongs the ooze. BCCs are locally destructive but very rarely metastasise, so the bleeding is a mechanical problem, not a marker of aggressive spread. Discriminators: pearly translucency and arborising vessels favour BCC over the hyperkeratotic/crusted, often tender SCC; slow multi-year growth with rolled borders argues against the rapid change of (amelanotic) melanoma; two-year chronicity argues against a pyogenic granuloma (which erupts over weeks). Management is mechanical haemostasis, protection, and a definite biopsy/excision pathway, while keeping melanoma/SCC on the differential.

differential.evidence_basis: Pearly nodule with rolled border, arborising vessels, central ulceration on sun-exposed nose.
Sun-exposed ulcerated lesion — SCC possible; less pearly, more hyperkeratotic. Biopsy differentiates.
Must be excluded in any changing/ulcerated lesion; slow rolled-border growth argues against but biopsy confirms.
Bleeds readily but typically erupts over weeks, not 2 years.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise likely BCC, keep melanoma/SCC on the differential and arrange biopsy; provide mechanical haemostasis without over-triaging as aggressive cancer.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic morphology, slow non-pigmented growth; bleeding mechanical and aspirin-related.
Lower probability but not excludable without histology — biopsy required.

differential.diagnosis: Nodulo-ulcerative basal cell carcinoma (confirm on biopsy)
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis: haemostasis + protection now; biopsy/excision pathway within 1–2 weeks.

red_flags.description: Features suggesting melanoma/aggressive lesion: rapid change, dark/variegated pigment, rapid growth
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise melanoma/SCC concern and change urgency; absent (slow, pearly, non-pigmented). Biopsy still required.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A misjudged melanoma could be under-prioritised.

red_flags.description: Uncontrolled bleeding (soaked dressing dripping despite 20 min firm pressure) on antiplatelet therapy
red_flags.clinical_significance: On aspirin, persistent facial bleeding may need ED cautery/haemostasis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Escalating to emergency oncology for a slowly bleeding BCC that needs routine biopsy.
Calling it 'just a BCC' remotely with no biopsy, potentially missing melanoma/SCC.
Not accounting for aspirin in the bleeding or the haemostasis advice.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Firm direct pressure ± in-person cautery for haemostasis; protective dressing; arrange biopsy/excision within 1–2 weeks; ED only if bleeding uncontrolled.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Remote reassurance without a biopsy pathway risks missing melanoma/SCC; emergency escalation for controllable bleeding is over-triage.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspected BCC should be confirmed histologically and treated (excision or other modality); BCC rarely metastasises but is locally destructive, especially on the nose.
